era_context: The Western Roman Empire is in acute crisis. Alaric I, king of the Visigoths, has been operating within and against Roman territory for over a decade, seeking land and recognition. In 408, he marches on Rome itself, beginning a siege that will last until 410. The usurper Constantine III controls Gaul and Hispania. The legitimate Western Roman Emperor Honorius is isolated in Ravenna, unable to mount an effective military response. The Eastern Roman Empire under Theodosius II is more stable but increasingly independent. This is the beginning of the end of the Western Roman Empire as a unified political entity.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine (sparse documentation)
East Asia (contemporary Chinese sources may exist but are underrepresented)

events.title: Alaric I marches on Rome; siege begins
events.description: In 408, Alaric I, king of the Visigoths, marched on Rome with his army, beginning a siege that would last until 410. Alaric had been operating in the Balkans and Italy for over a decade, seeking land, money, and recognition from the Roman Empire. His march on Rome was motivated by failed negotiations with Emperor Honorius and the assassination of the Roman general Stilicho, who had been Alaric's primary negotiating partner. The siege cut off Rome's grain supplies and forced the city into a state of crisis. Alaric demanded tribute and land; Honorius, isolated in Ravenna, was unable or unwilling to meet these demands. The siege would continue through 409 and culminate in the sack of Rome in August 410.
key_figures: Stilicho (deceased, but his death precipitated the crisis)
Olympius (Roman general, rival to Stilicho's faction)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Augustine of Hippo, City of God (written 413–426, but referencing events of 408–410)

events.certainty_note: Alaric's march on Rome in 408 is attested in multiple sources. The exact date and sequence of events are less certain, but the occurrence of the siege is confirmed.
cross_references: Follows from earlier Visigothic conflicts with Rome (395–408)

events.title: Assassination of Stilicho
events.description: In 408, the Roman general Stilicho, who had been the dominant military and political figure in the Western Roman Empire for nearly two decades, was executed on the orders of Emperor Honorius. Stilicho had been the primary negotiator with Alaric and had maintained a precarious balance between military defense and political accommodation. His death removed the most capable military leader and the most experienced negotiator with the Visigoths. The execution was orchestrated by Olympius, a rival faction at court, and was motivated by suspicions (likely unfounded) that Stilicho was plotting with the barbarians. Stilicho's death precipitated Alaric's march on Rome, as the Visigothic king lost his primary negotiating partner.
key_figures: Olympius (Roman general, orchestrator of Stilicho's death)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Claudian, Panegyricus dictus Honorio (written before 408, but referencing Stilicho)

events.certainty_note: Stilicho's execution in 408 is well attested in multiple sources. The date, circumstances, and consequences are documented.
cross_references: Part of broader political instability in the Western Roman Empire

events.title: Constantine III consolidates control of Gaul and Hispania
events.description: Constantine III, a Roman general proclaimed emperor by his troops in Britain in 407, had by 408 consolidated control over much of Gaul and Hispania. He ruled from Arles and maintained the apparatus of imperial government. However, his authority was contested by the legitimate Western Roman Emperor Honorius and was increasingly challenged by barbarian movements. Constantine III's regime represented the fragmentation of imperial authority in the western provinces and would eventually collapse by 411.
key_figures: Gerontius (Roman general, initially supporting Constantine III)

sources.name: Orosius, Seven Books of History Against the Pagans
Numismatic evidence: coins minted in Constantine III's name

events.certainty_note: Constantine III's rule in Gaul and Hispania is well attested in numismatic and literary sources.
cross_references: Follows from 407-001 (Constantine III's proclamation in Britain)

events.title: Theodosius II becomes Eastern Roman Emperor
events.description: In 408, Theodosius II, age seven, became Eastern Roman Emperor following the death of his father Arcadius. Theodosius II would reign for 42 years (408–450) and would be responsible for major legal and administrative reforms, including the Theodosian Code. His reign was marked by relative stability in the Eastern Empire, in contrast to the Western Empire's collapse. Theodosius II was initially under the guardianship of his sister Pulcheria and the praetorian prefect Anthemius.
events.certainty_note: Theodosius II's accession in 408 is well documented in contemporary legislation and near-contemporary chronicles.
cross_references: Part of broader Eastern Roman stability in the late 4th and early 5th centuries

disconfirming_evidence: The narrative of Stilicho's assassination as a catastrophic turning point has been questioned by some modern scholars. While Stilicho's death was certainly significant, the Western Roman Empire's military and political problems were deeper and more structural than the loss of a single general. The empire had been facing military pressure on multiple frontiers for decades, and the financial and administrative systems were already strained. Additionally, the characterization of Stilicho as uniquely capable may be exaggerated; other generals and administrators of the period were also competent, though perhaps less well documented. The 'catastrophe' narrative may reflect the perspective of later sources (Zosimus, Orosius) rather than the reality of 408.
historiographic_note: The year 408 is moderately well documented for events in the Mediterranean and Italy, but sources are unevenly distributed. Zosimus and Orosius are the primary sources for Alaric's march and Stilicho's assassination, but both are writing decades later. Contemporary written records are sparse; most information comes from later compilations and chronicles. Archaeological evidence is limited for this year. Events in Britain, Gaul, northern Europe, and beyond the Roman frontier are less thoroughly documented. The sources are heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean and Roman perspective, particularly toward the court at Ravenna.

graph_edges.note: Alaric's march on Rome in 408 was precipitated by Stilicho's assassination earlier that year.
The march on Rome in 408 led to the siege that continued through 409.
Stilicho's assassination removed the primary negotiator with Alaric, precipitating the march on Rome.
Constantine III's consolidation of power in 408 led to his eventual defeat in 411.
Theodosius II's accession in 408 led to his promulgation of the Theodosian Code in 438.
